issue_title: Unexpected CancelledError raised via TCPConnector._create_direct_connection
issue_body:
## Long story short

During the aiohttp.ClientSession.get method, an unexpected CancelledError is raised via TCPConnector._create_direct_connection method (much like https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/1686). My feeling is that some kind of state corruption triggers the CancelledError. If I close the ClientSession and create a new one, then it suppresses the problem.

## Expected behaviour

I expect either a response, asyncio.TimeoutError, or aiohttp.ClientError.

## Actual behaviour

Some change that came after aiohttp-1.3.3 started triggering this unexpected CancelledError. I first noticed with aiohttp-1.3.5, and found that rolling back to aiohttp-1.3.3 suppressed the problem.  I have also experienced a similar problem with aiohttp-2.0.7. With aiohttp-2.0.7, I am able to suppress the problem by catching the CancelledError and creating a new ClientSession instance. Here is a traceback with aiohttp-2.0.7 and python-3.5.3 on Linux:
```
  File "client.py", line 110, in _get
    resp = await client_session.get(url, timeout=30, params=params)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/client.py", line 621, in __await__
    resp = yield from self._coro
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/client.py", line 225, in _request
    conn = yield from self._connector.connect(req)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/connector.py", line 375, in connect
    proto = yield from self._create_connection(req)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/connector.py", line 632, in _create_connection
    _, proto = yield from self._create_direct_connection(req)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/connector.py", line 655, in _create_direct_connection
    local_addr=self._local_addr)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/base_events.py", line 729, in create_connection
    yield from tasks.wait(fs, loop=self)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/tasks.py", line 355, in wait
    return (yield from _wait(fs, timeout, return_when, loop))
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/tasks.py", line 438, in _wait
    yield from waiter
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/futures.py", line 380, in __iter__
    yield self  # This tells Task to wait for completion.
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/tasks.py", line 304, in _wakeup
    future.result()
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/futures.py", line 285, in result
    raise CancelledError
concurrent.futures._base.CancelledError
```
I got this similar exception with aiohttp-1.3.5 and python-3.5.2:
```
  File "client.py", line 110, in _get
    resp = await client_session.get(url, timeout=30, params=params)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/client.py", line 590, in __await__
    resp = yield from self._coro
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/client.py", line 217, in _request
    conn = yield from self._connector.connect(req)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/connector.py", line 370, in connect
    yield from self._create_connection(req)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/connector.py", line 663, in _create_connection
    transport, proto = yield from self._create_direct_connection(req)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/connector.py", line 686, in _create_direct_connection
    local_addr=self._local_addr)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/base_events.py", line 649, in create_connection
    yield from tasks.wait(fs, loop=self)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/tasks.py", line 347, in wait
    return (yield from _wait(fs, timeout, return_when, loop))
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/tasks.py", line 430, in _wait
    yield from waiter
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/futures.py", line 361, in __iter__
    yield self  # This tells Task to wait for completion.
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/tasks.py", line 296, in _wakeup
    future.result()
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/futures.py", line 266, in result
    raise CancelledError
concurrent.futures._base.CancelledError
```
The problem was not reproducible with aiohttp-1.3.3.

## Steps to reproduce

Like issue https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/1686, this issue may be difficult to reproduce. My code creates sessions for many different hosts simultaneously, using a separate ClientSession instance that is re-used for many connections to the same host. A new ClientSession is created in the event of an asyncio.TimeoutError, an aiohttp.ClientError, or a response status which is not in the 4xx range. The code that processes many requests to a particular host:port can be summarized as follows:
```python
def new_session():
    return aiohttp.ClientSession(connector=aiohttp.TCPConnector())

client_session = new_session()
for url, params in requests:
    try:
        resp = await client_session.get(url, timeout=30, params=params)
        await resp.read()
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        client_session.close()
        client_session = new_session()
    except aiohttp.ClientError:
        client_session.close()
        client_session = new_session()
    else:
        if resp.status < 400 or resp.status >= 500:
            client_session.close()
            client_session = new_session()
```
I can suppress the problem like this:
```
    try:
        resp = await client_session.get(url, timeout=30, params=params)
    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        client_session.close()
        client_session = new_session()
        resp = await client_session.get(url, timeout=30, params=params)
```

## Your environment

I am using aiohttp-2.0.7 and python-3.5.3, with a Linux 4.4.35 kernel. Requests are directed at http endpoints on many different hosts (running a proprietary java application).

issue_title: jacobian not working when np.prod is used and one element is 0.
issue_body:
Hi, I am starting to use autograd for my own project and it's an amazing tool!

I encountered an issue very early during my tests, where the jacobian fails to be computed if I use the np.prod function, and only in the particular case when one entry is zero.  Here is a minimal working example of the problem:

```python
# import numpy as np
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import jacobian


def fun1(x):
    return np.array([x[0], x[1], x[0] * x[1]])


def fun2(x):
    return np.array([x[0], x[1], x.prod()])

fun1_jac = jacobian(fun1)
fun2_jac = jacobian(fun2)

x = np.array([0., 2.])  # when there is no zero element, both methods work.

print 'fun1:'
print fun1(x)
print fun1_jac(x)

print 'fun2:'
print fun2(x)
print fun2_jac(x)
```

I'm really new to this package, and only started using it yesterday, so I hope I have not misused or overlook any feature which would explain the above.

issue_title: Is it really a good idea for the imports list to be separate?
issue_body:
This is a consistent question I get when I demo peru for people. Why not put the import path for a module in the module's declaration? There are two decent reasons and one bad reason:

1. It's nice to be able to look in one place and see everything that peru is going to do when you sync.
2. Imports are not necessarily one-module-one-path. A module could be imported multiple times with different rules.
3. Bad reason: this is a holdover from when remote modules were more involved, with potentially their own imports, when some symmetry between the remote modules and the toplevel module made sense.

Allowing import paths as part of a module declaration definitely simplifies the hello world example. I could go either way on examples that are more complicated than that. I *really* want to avoid having two different ways to do the same thing, like allowing both an imports list and inline import paths. I think the biggest question for me right now is whether point (1) is really true, or whether I just think it's true because I'm used to it...

issue_title: Location of screenshots and xdist
issue_body: It seems that if xdist is active then `--splinter-screenshots-dir` has no effect (they still go into the `session_timpdir`). Why is it like that? Can this behavior change, or at least allow customization?

issue_title: Consistent error-handling in python API functions
issue_body: API functions should return an object when calling create, for the new "thing" created, otherwise raise an error.  The current code is inconsistent in that it returns True or False for some errors and raise for others.  The caller cannot tell from a False return what went wrong.

issue_title: exec_cmd needs rework due to same issue as in #411
issue_body:
We have a regression/omission in exec_cmd and its clones of #411 

eg.

```

>>> a = mr.tm.util.bash
>>> check = a.exec_cmd(
            'run', utilCmdArgs='-c "diff /var/local/scf/test '
                               '/var/local/scf/test"')
>>> hasattr(check, 'commandResult')
False
>>> check2 = a.exec_cmd(
            'run', utilCmdArgs='-c "diff /var/local/scf/test '
                               '/var/local/scf/test2"')

>>> hasattr(check2, 'commandResult')
True
>>> hasattr(check, 'commandResult')
True


(...)

>>> a = mr.tm.util.bash
>>> b = mr.tm.util.bash
>>> check = a.exec_cmd(
            'run', utilCmdArgs='-c "diff /var/local/scf/test '
                               '/var/local/scf/test"')
>>> hasattr(check, 'commandResult')
False
>>> hasattr(check, 'commandResult')
False
>>> check2 = b.exec_cmd(
            'run', utilCmdArgs='-c "diff /var/local/scf/test '
                               '/var/local/scf/test2"')
>>> hasattr(check2, 'commandResult')
True
>>> hasattr(check, 'commandResult')
True
>>> type(check2)
<class 'f5.bigip.tm.util.Bash.Bash'>
>>> check is check2
True

```

since the last v1.3.5 release, we have issues using the lib with python3.4

return self._create(**kwargs)

issue_body:
# Issue Title
 Can't add new migration using startup project with netCore2.1 getting null on connection string param

# General
Example:
In the following repository I'm using a startup project to initiate a connection string to a dbContext.
it's working with 2.0  but not with netCore2.1, code base is the same only the target framework and libraries have changed to 2.1

https://github.com/alentor/Bloggerium

The code base it one to one, the only differences is that I'm targeting netcoreapp2.1 and using 
Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore 2.1 instead of 2.0.3
Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design 2.1 instead of 2.0.3
Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.SqlServer 2.1 instead of 2.0.3


For 2.0 and 2.1 using dotnet cli 3.1.300 and netcore ef 2.1.0-rtm-30799
windows 10 version 1803 build 17134.81

issue_title: Using .NET Core Libary from .NET Framework App
issue_body:
Is there any way to use .NET Core Library from .NET Console App in same solution.

Example:
1) Create .NET Core Library project, add some class to test. Set frameworks to
`"frameworks": {
  "net45": { },
  "netstandard1.5": {  "imports": "dnxcore50"  }
}`

2) Create .NET45 console app in same project.
3) Try to reference library from console app. It's ok.
4) Try to use class from library in console app. You will get an error that class can't be found.
You can try add library namespace manually, but it will not help (same error for namespaces).

Workaround:
5) Remove reference to library project
6) Add reference to compiled library DLL for .NET45
7) All works!

Look like bug?

issue_title: schema2parameters - body + header
issue_body:
Hey,

I have a schema and the default_in = body, but one of the fields has a headers location.
schema2parameters inserts the header field inside the body instead of returning one body param and one header param.

What do I do?

``` python
class AccountsSchema(Schema):
    user_id = fields.Int(required=True, location="headers")
    name = fields.Str(required=True, validate=validate.Length(min=1, max=255), location="json")
    status = fields.Str(required=True, validate=validate.OneOf(choices=["Active", "Paused"]), location="json")
```



``` python
def fields2parameters(fields, schema=None, spec=None, use_refs=True, dump=True,
                      default_in='body', name='body', required=False):
    """Return an array of OpenAPI parameters given a mapping between field names and
    :class:`Field <marshmallow.Field>` objects. If `default_in` is "body", then return an array
    of a single parameter; else return an array of a parameter for each included field in
    the :class:`Schema <marshmallow.Schema>`.

    https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#parameterObject
    """
    if default_in == 'body':
        if schema is not None:
            # Prevent circular import
            from apispec.ext.marshmallow import resolve_schema_dict
            prop = resolve_schema_dict(spec, schema, dump=dump)
        else:
            prop = fields2jsonschema(fields, spec=spec, use_refs=use_refs, dump=dump)

        return [{
            'in': default_in,
            'required': required,
            'name': name,
            'schema': prop,
        }]

    assert not getattr(schema, 'many', False), \
        "Schemas with many=True are only supported for 'json' location (aka 'in: body')"

    exclude_fields = getattr(getattr(schema, 'Meta', None), 'exclude', [])

    return [
        field2parameter(
            field_obj,
            name=_observed_name(field_obj, field_name),
            spec=spec,
            use_refs=use_refs,
            dump=dump,
            default_in=default_in
        )
            for field_name, field_obj in iteritems(fields)
            if (
                (field_name not in exclude_fields)
                and not (field_obj.dump_only and not dump)
            )
    ]
```

issue_title: Read does not honor the timeout on Linux
issue_body:
Hello,
  Reading from the serial port with a timeout is not reliable on my system:
* Linux Mint Nadia
* Python 2.7.3
* pyserial 2.7 (but also 2.5) installed both with ```pip``` and the latest tar.gz

I have a CDC-ACM dongle that transmits data periodically.

This simple program does not respect the timeout:

```
import serial
import time

ser = serial.Serial(
                    port        = '/dev/ttyACM0',
                    baudrate    = 460800,
                    bytesize    = serial.EIGHTBITS,
                    parity      = serial.PARITY_NONE,
                    stopbits    = serial.STOPBITS_ONE,
                    timeout     = 0.1
                   )

while True:
   length = 1000000

   t0 = time.time()
   print str(len(ser.read(length))) + ' ' + str((time.time() - t0))
```

Running the above I get:

```
123 0.109111070633
246 0.205754995346
123 0.106971025467
369 0.310961008072
123 0.106863975525
369 0.309740066528
246 0.214066982269
```
Data is read but with an higher latency.
Things go worst if the timeout is set to 1s, in which case data is read with higher latencies (>> 1s).

What's going on? For comparison I've tried the following program that mimics what ```pyserial``` should do:

```
import serial
import time
import select

ser = serial.Serial(
                    port        = '/dev/ttyACM0',
                    baudrate    = 460800,
                    bytesize    = serial.EIGHTBITS,
                    parity      = serial.PARITY_NONE,
                    stopbits    = serial.STOPBITS_ONE,
                    timeout     = None, #block forever
                   )

def read(ser, length, timeout):
   n = 0
   b = bytearray()
   ser.timeout = 0 #non-blocking
   while timeout >= 0 and n < length:
       t0 = time.time()
       ready = select.select([ser], [], [], timeout)
       timeout -= (time.time() - t0)
       if len(ready[0]) != 0:
           bb = ser.read(length - n)
           n += len(bb)
           b.extend(bb)
   return b

while True:
   timeout = 0.1 #seconds
   length = 1000000

   t0 = time.time()
   b = read(ser, length, timeout)
   print str(len(b)) + ' ' + str((time.time() - t0))
```

Whose output is:

```
123 0.101263999939
123 0.101146936417
123 0.101223945618
123 0.100612163544
123 0.100650072098
123 0.101171016693
123 0.10132598877
```

As you can see data is consistently read with the specified latency.
Other runs:

timeout=2.0:

```
2460 2.0148499012
2337 2.01164889336
2340 2.01203608513
2337 2.01602220535
2337 2.01417803764
2337 2.01304197311
```

timeout=2.0, length=10:

```
10 0.000165939331055
10 0.000646114349365
10 0.000133991241455
10 9.58442687988e-05
10 0.000693082809448
10 0.000132083892822
10 0.000753879547119
```

issue_title: how to implement Sparse Fieldsets?
issue_body:
I would like the Sparse Fieldsets feature:
http://jsonapi.org/format/#fetching-sparse-fieldsets

I tried to implement it and thought creating a custom serializer and override [rest_framework.serializers.Serializer.fields](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/8cae462b3a3d724cf83fd33572c0ea23f91f2278/rest_framework/serializers.py#L318-L330) property.

However, we need the `resource_name` to get the `fields[TYPE]` query parameter value (TYPE=resource_name).
I find [rest_framework_json_api.utils.get_resource_name](https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/blob/f55076ea92a08a845c09fbd63f3976d264fbb984/rest_framework_json_api/utils.py#L33-L77), however its argument `rendrerer_context` is created after the serializer returns data. For example, in ListModelMixin, `rendered_context` is created in `Response(serializer.data)` at https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/8cae462b3a3d724cf83fd33572c0ea23f91f2278/rest_framework/mixins.py#L47-L48)

So how should we implement the Sparse Fieldsets feature?

import sys

As was mentioned in [#114](https://github.com/core-api/python-client/issues/114) there are a few important topics to consider:

2. What does an example auth flow look like?
3. What information does the scheme itself need to present in order to make that possible?

issue_title: Feature request: expose finite sorts to the Java API
issue_body: There's currently no way to create finite sorts from Java, so all finite types must be encoded as bitvectors sorts of some size, and constants must be arbitrary bitvector constants.  Since this is already implemented at the C++ level, it would be great if this was exposed to Java.

issue_title: Build fails on certain ARMv7 linux environments demanding -fPIC
issue_body:
On some but not all of my ARMv7 linux machines, linking fails with:
```
/usr/bin/ld: api/dll/mem_initializer.o: relocation R_ARM_THM_MOVW_ABS_NC against `a local symbol' can not be used when making a shared object; recompile with -fPIC
api/dll/mem_initializer.o: error adding symbols: Bad value
```
I get this failure on an Nvidia Jetson-TK1 devkit running their provided Ubuntu 14.04 and g++ 4.8.4 and on a https://www.scaleway.com/ server running Ubuntu 15.10 and g++ 5.2.1. It builds properly on a Raspberry Pi running Raspbian 8.0 (jessie) and g++ 4.9.2.

I have a patch that fixes it by adding -fPIC when building on armv7 but I want to think if there is a more fine-grained solution here, since it doesn't affect all my machines.

issue_title: What is the status of the optimization stuff in the unstable branch?
issue_body:
Sorry for opening an issue, but I couldn't find any other way to contact the development team (there is a "contact us" link at http://research.microsoft.com/en-us/um/redmond/projects/z3/, but it just downloads some document about Windows Small Business Server 2003 Service Pack 1). 

What is the status on the optimization stuff in the unstable branch? Is there a timeline on when it will make it to a stable release? 

If it won't be there soon, is there a way to do optimization in the latest stable release with reasonable performance (I know that at worst I can set up a SAT problem and bisect it).

The kind of problem that I wish to solve is a pseudo-boolean problem. I have several SAT clauses and a linear expression with integer coefficients on some of the SAT variables which I wish to minimize. 

Specifically, I'm investigating Z3 for use in [conda](http://conda.pydata.org/) for dependency resolution. Conda already uses a SAT solver (picosat) to do dependency resolution, but the pseudo-boolean constraints are converted to SAT using BDDs (see "Translating Pseudo-Boolean Constraints into SAT", Eén and Söensson 2006). The SAT clauses represent things like "package A depends on package B", and the pseudo-boolean formula that is minimized lets the solver find a solution that has maximal versions installed. The formula is then minimized using bisection. 

The problem with conda's approach is that it is too slow for certain problems (it can take minutes to get a solution). We've looked at using an ILP solver, but there don't seem to be any good free ones for Python. I tried using Z3's optimization stuff (see https://github.com/asmeurer/conda/blob/clauses/Z3Opt.ipynb) to solve a problem that takes conda's current solver about 15 minutes to solve, and it solved it in a few seconds, which is plenty fast, and I haven't even made some optimizations yet (e.g., there are some carnality constrants that are represented in CNF, but could probably be used by Z3 more efficiently as actual cardinality constraints). (side note, why does it take 14 seconds to convert the clauses to Z3 Python objects?)

conda's current solver is a hassle to users when it hits a hard problem, and is preventing us from doing some things that we'd like to do with the solver. Z3 seems like it's a good fit, since it has Python bindings, works in Python 2 and 3 and all platforms, and is MIT open source.

issue_title: recursive __call__ instead of layers.get_output
issue_body:
I've just started to play with lasagne, please apologize my shallow understanding of the code. 

The usage of `lasagne.layers.get_output` is kind of counter intuitive to me. It uses `get_all_layers` to navigate through the code with a queue. Wouldn't it be more compact to add the following recursive function to the `Layer` class (and overriding it in `MergeLayer`) instead?
```python
    def __call__(self,inputs, **kwargs):
        if self.input_layer is not None:
            inputs = self.input_layer(inputs, **kwargs)
        return self.get_output_for(self,inputs,**kwargs)
```
This way you could replace 
```python
lasagne.layers.get_output(output_layer, X_batch)
```
by
```python
output_layer(X_batch)
```

While it's not a big difference, It feels more theanoish to me. It also makes the code more compact and avoids the usage of `isinstance` and `hasattr`. Am I missing some (many) edge cases ?

In the pynwb documentation:

from pynwb import NWBHDF5IO

from pynwb import NWBHDF5IO

io = NWBHDF5IO('basic_example.nwb', mode='w')

issue_title: Add some predicates for strings
issue_body:
I'd like to add a few predicates that are specialized for strings, like `are.containing`.  It will make the predicate API a little more elaborate, which is unfortunate.  Since we're going to try hiding booleans from students for the first month+, I think the flexibility we gain in working with text is worth the cost.

Use case: Right now I'm trying to write an exercise about news articles, and I'd like to find all the articles whose titles contain the word "Ecuador".  The best workaround right now is something like 

`def ecuador_location(string): return string.find("Ecuador")`
`tbl.with_column("Ecuador location", tbl.apply(ecuador_location, "Title").where("Ecuador location", are.above(-1))`

...which is pretty ugly, and not really easier to understand than boolean values.

issue_title: Support more key shortcuts
issue_body:
Right now you have to use the mouse to execute one cell/all cells/all cells below (at least on Windows).
It would be nice to support key bindings (Fx keys + modifiers ?) in order to quickly run the current cell (and the other combinations), clear the output, delete cell, re-order cell, and more.
In fact, I would like nteract to be as keyboard friendly as possible.

issue_title: iframe entire display area
issue_body:
As brought up in a call with @craigcitro and others today about colab and interop with nteract, their iframe output areas are incredibly performant, even on huge notebooks. They do rely on service workers to work, so they come with the caveat that it will only work in certain browsers. For our targets, that's not a big problem. This will cause issues for libraries like Bokeh, though I think we can cross that bridge.

I'm filing this issue to say that I'd love to switch to iframes by default in the desktop and all (\*) web frontends, so that we can keep them secure by default regardless of kernel. I know Jupyter in general has been reluctant to adopt this. I think we can do it, keeping people more secure in general.

if __name__ == '__main__':

```

issue_title: self-modifying code and angr
issue_body:
Hi,

Consider the following self-modifying x86 code:

    00000000004006af <f>:
      4006af:   31 c0                   xor    %eax,%eax
      4006b1:   b9 0a 00 00 00          mov    $0xa,%ecx

    00000000004006b6 <L>:
      4006b6:   89 0d 01 00 00 00       mov    %ecx,0x1(%rip)        # 4006bd <L+0x7>
      4006bc:   05 78 56 34 12          add    $0x12345678,%eax
      4006c1:   ff c9                   dec    %ecx
      4006c3:   75 f1                   jne    4006b6 <L>
      4006c5:   c3                      retq

Assume that this code is inside a writable+executable section. Unfortunately, there are few problems when analyzing this code using angr (notice that `support_selfmodifying_code=True` is always used):

- if `opt_level>=1` (VEX optimazione level) and `num_inst!=1` then this x86 code is wrongly translated into:
   [when pc = 0x4006af]

      IRSB {
               t0:Ity_I32 t1:Ity_I32 t2:Ity_I32 t3:Ity_I64 t4:Ity_I32 t5:Ity_I32 t6:Ity_I32 t7:Ity_I32 t8:Ity_I32 t9:Ity_I64 t10:Ity_I32 t11:Ity_I64 t12:Ity_I32 t13:Ity_I64 t14:Ity_I64 t15:Ity_I64 t16:Ity_I64 t17:Ity_I32 t18:Ity_I64 t19:Ity_I32 t20:Ity_I64 t21:Ity_I64 t22:Ity_I64 t23:Ity_I64 t24:Ity_I32 t25:Ity_I64 t26:Ity_I64 t27:Ity_I64 t28:Ity_I64 t29:Ity_I64 t30:Ity_I64 t31:Ity_I64 t32:Ity_I64 t33:Ity_I1 t34:Ity_I64 t35:Ity_I64 t36:Ity_I64 t37:Ity_I64 t38:Ity_I64 t39:Ity_I64 t40:Ity_I64 t41:Ity_I64 t42:Ity_I1 t43:Ity_I32 t44:Ity_I1
       00 | ------ IMark(0x4006af, 2, 0) ------
       01 | ------ IMark(0x4006b1, 5, 0) ------
       02 | PUT(pc) = 0x00000000004006b6
       03 | ------ IMark(0x4006b6, 6, 0) ------
       04 | STle(0x00000000004006bd) = 0x0000000a
       05 | ------ IMark(0x4006bc, 5, 0) ------
       06 | PUT(rax) = 0x0000000012345678
       07 | ------ IMark(0x4006c1, 2, 0) ------
       08 | t29 = GET:I64(cc_ndep)
       09 | t40 = amd64g_calculate_rflags_c(0x0000000000000003,0x0000000000000000,0x0000000012345678,t29):Ity_I64
       10 | t30 = t40
       11 | PUT(cc_ndep) = t30
       12 | PUT(cc_op) = 0x000000000000001b
       13 | PUT(cc_dep1) = 0x0000000000000009
       14 | PUT(cc_dep2) = 0x0000000000000000
       15 | PUT(rcx) = 0x0000000000000009
       16 | PUT(pc) = 0x00000000004006c3
       17 | ------ IMark(0x4006c3, 2, 0) ------
       18 | t43 = 64to32(0x0000000000000009)
       19 | t42 = CmpEQ32(t43,0x00000000)
       20 | t41 = 1Uto64(t42)
       21 | t38 = t41
       22 | t44 = 64to1(t38)
       23 | t33 = t44
       24 | if (t33) { PUT(rip) = 0x4006c5L; Ijk_Boring }
       NEXT: PUT(rip) = 0x00000000004006b6; Ijk_Boring
       }
       
   [after first jump at the end of BB, when pc = 0x4006b6]

       IRSB {
       t0:Ity_I64 t1:Ity_I32 t2:Ity_I32 t3:Ity_I32 t4:Ity_I32 t5:Ity_I32 t6:Ity_I32 t7:Ity_I64 t8:Ity_I32 t9:Ity_I64 t10:Ity_I64 t11:Ity_I64 t12:Ity_I64 t13:Ity_I32 t14:Ity_I64 t15:Ity_I64 t16:Ity_I64 t17:Ity_I64 t18:Ity_I64 t19:Ity_I64 t20:Ity_I64 t21:Ity_I64 t22:Ity_I1 t23:Ity_I64 t24:Ity_I64 t25:Ity_I64 t26:Ity_I64 t27:Ity_I64 t28:Ity_I64 t29:Ity_I32 t30:Ity_I32 t31:Ity_I64 t32:Ity_I64 t33:Ity_I32 t34:Ity_I64 t35:Ity_I64 t36:Ity_I64 t37:Ity_I64 t38:Ity_I1 t39:Ity_I32 t40:Ity_I1

       00 | ------ IMark(0x4006b6, 6, 0) ------
       01 | t7 = GET:I64(rcx)
       02 | t29 = 64to32(t7)
       03 | t6 = t29
       04 | STle(0x00000000004006bd) = t6
       05 | ------ IMark(0x4006bc, 5, 0) ------
       06 | t9 = GET:I64(rax)
       07 | t30 = 64to32(t9)
       08 | t8 = t30
       09 | t3 = Add32(t8,0x0000000a)
       10 | t31 = 32Uto64(t8)
       11 | t10 = t31
       12 | t32 = 32Uto64(t3)
       13 | t12 = t32
       14 | PUT(rax) = t12
       15 | ------ IMark(0x4006c1, 2, 0) ------
       16 | t33 = 64to32(t7)
       17 | t13 = t33
       18 | t5 = Sub32(t13,0x00000001)
       19 | t18 = GET:I64(cc_ndep)
       20 | t34 = amd64g_calculate_rflags_c(0x0000000000000003,t10,0x000000000000000a,t18):Ity_I64
       21 | t19 = t34
       22 | PUT(cc_ndep) = t19
       23 | PUT(cc_op) = 0x000000000000001b
       24 | t35 = 32Uto64(t5)
       25 | t20 = t35
       26 | PUT(cc_dep1) = t20
       27 | PUT(cc_dep2) = 0x0000000000000000
       28 | t36 = 32Uto64(t5)
       29 | t21 = t36
       30 | PUT(rcx) = t21
       31 | PUT(pc) = 0x00000000004006c3
       32 | ------ IMark(0x4006c3, 2, 0) ------
       33 | t39 = 64to32(t20)
       34 | t38 = CmpEQ32(t39,0x00000000)
       35 | t37 = 1Uto64(t38)
       36 | t27 = t37
       37 | t40 = 64to1(t27)
       38 | t22 = t40
       39 | if (t22) { PUT(rip) = 0x4006c5L; Ijk_Boring }
       NEXT: PUT(rip) = 0x00000000004006b6; Ijk_Boring
       }

which will produce a very different result/execution. In particular, it will loop almost indefinitely. Notice that even Valgrind returns a wrong result. I think VEX is making some wrong assumptions when lifting this x86 code (e.g., when doing some optimization passes).

- if  `opt_level=0` or  `num_inst=1`: it will not loop indefinitely but still returns a wrong result (`eax=0x64`). Notice that when `num_inst=1`, pyVEX will not "wrongly" translate this x86 code since the optimization scope is too small (a single instruction, instead of a BB).

-  if  (`opt_level=0` or  `num_inst=1`) and `translation_cache=False`: we get the correct result

Some observations:
- `support_selfmodifying_code=True` does not seem to guarantee a correct execution in presence of self-modifying code
- when `support_selfmodifying_code=True`, `opt_level` should be set to `0`. Alternatively, angr doc should warn the user about possible wrong translations. I think that this a bug of Valgrind but they probably will not care since Valgrind is not designed for testing self-modifying code. On the other hand, angr aims even at this target (this is my intuition, correct me if I am wrong).
- when `support_selfmodifying_code=True`, `translation_cache` should be automatically set to `False`.

What do you think?

Let me know if you want my angr script and/or a working binary.

issue_title: Bug in CFGFast
issue_body:
I compiled coreutils 8.27. In the **cp** program, function 0x402FB0 (target_directory_operand), CFGFast doesn't recover the CFG correctly.  Below is the binary:
[cp.zip](https://github.com/angr/angr/files/1262088/cp.zip)

The error is a very simple one. Here is the CFG recovered by angr:
![screenshot from 2017-08-29 17-36-44](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2527873/29850200-bd7c2cd4-8ce0-11e7-8db1-cccec109b99f.png)

Here is the CFG recovered by IDA, which is the correct one:
![screenshot from 2017-08-29 17-37-04](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2527873/29850213-d23f9782-8ce0-11e7-88e0-28b0ed7b898e.png)

As you can see, there is an edge missing in the angr's CFG, from node `0x402FEA` to node `0x402FF1`. Angr's analysis basically put a duplicated node `0x402FF1` inside the node `0x402FFA` (since the first one contains return statement). The node `0x402FEA` suppose to be 7 byte instead of 18.

issue_title: Cache data vs fully initialized Job
issue_body:
This is an issue to remind me to look into the use of Cached data on an object from the .idx file vs loading the full object description from the .xml.

Currently this feels like it's extremely difficult to follow where the actual data is being stored and where it's being accessed.
I've made some improvements with the optimisation work for 6.1.14 but this highlights that this needs addressing properly imo.

I've some ideas on how the code could be cleaned up with regards to this but it should probably be done for 6.1.15 or onwards. Basically I want to remove the overhead in trying to access data that isn't there and make it much clearer where and when a job should be fully loaded from disk in future.
This goes hand in hand with improving the SubJobXMLList which unfortunately is still loading ALL subjobs for many tasks due to some parts of the codebase requesting the whole subjob or all subjobs before it knows what it wants to do with the information.

issue_title: Crash on records list endpoint when the collection is empty and readonly is true
issue_body:
When the collection has no record and was never reached, a timestamp is stored.

This does not work if `readonly` is true because the user does not have the permission to insert into the timestamp table.

We should not try to bump the timestamp when `readonly` is true.

See:
* Kinto/kinto#433
* https://github.com/mozilla-services/cliquet/pull/649

issue_title: Wavelet coefficients to single array (and vice versa?)
issue_body:
In my subfield (signal processing), it's common to see the full level wavelet transforms represented in a single array. This adds indexing ease and wavelet transforms tend to be treated as complete arrays.

I'd like to see

``` python
x = np.random.rand(10)
coeffs = pywt.wavedec(x)
w = pywt.coeffs_to_array(coeffs)
# perform a variety of signal processing algorithms
# ...
coeffs_processed = pywt.array_to_coeffs()
y = pywt.waverec(x)
```

I see two options for an interface:

- defining two functions, something like `wavelet_coeffs_to_array` and `array_to_wavelet_coeffs`.
- adding a keyword argument to every function, something like `return_full_array`

We started using credstash last year and our automation for installing it failed after the move from PyCrypto to cryptography. ([link](https://github.com/fugue/credstash/commit/f039c63a07dd30cecdc0e6a93b3c9ae0713418c7))

To be able to quickly identify what has changed, recently would it be possible to keep a CHANGELOG.md file at the root of this repo?

Thanks.

issue_title: How to Identify Camera from USB?
issue_body:
| Required Info  |   |
|---|---|
| Camera Model | SR300 | 
| Firmware Version |  3.10.10.0 & 3.17.0.0 | 
| Operating System & Version |  Ubuntu 16.04 |
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)  |   4.4.0-62 |
| Build System |  Makefile | 

This issue might be impossible to solve. I have two SR300 cameras, one with 3.10.10.0 firmware and another with 3.17.0.0. I tried plug each camera into two separate ports. The one with the lower firmware reports two separate ports (7-1 and 7-2). Whereas the one with the higher firmware reports the same for both (7-1 and 7-1). I get this ports by calling `rs_get_device_usb_port_id` (which internally calls libusb). I understand [ports are not guaranteed to be unique](http://libusb.sourceforge.net/api-1.0/group__dev.html#ga14879a0ea7daccdcddb68852d86c00c4) however this is on the same hardware with only slightly different cameras.

In addition, running `v4l2-ctl --list-devices` gives two different formats (this is on hub-port 7-2): 

3.10.10.0
Intel(R) RealSense(TM) Camera S (**usb-0000:00:10.0-2**):
	/dev/video0
	/dev/video1

 3.17.0.0
Intel(R) RealSense(TM) Camera S (**usb-0000:00:10.0-2.1**):
	/dev/video0
	/dev/video1

Any idea why this behaviour is different and how to resolve it?

issue_title: too many presets
issue_body:
Some of the featurizers have a large amount of presets, all of which are doing essentially the same thing. Here's one from a recent PR, although there were many before this of the same form (some committed by me).

```
+        if preset == "VoronoiNN":
 +            return ChemicalSRO(VoronoiNN())
 +        elif preset == "JMolNN":
 +            return ChemicalSRO(JMolNN())
 +        elif preset == "MinimumDistanceNN":
 +            return ChemicalSRO(MinimumDistanceNN())
 +        elif preset == "MinimumOKeeffeNN":
 +            return ChemicalSRO(MinimumOKeeffeNN())
 +        elif preset == "MinimumVIRENN":
 +            return ChemicalSRO(MinimumVIRENN())
 +        else:
 +            raise RuntimeError('Unknown preset.')
```

We should:

1. Clean up the code to shorten it, using something like this:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/4821104/python-dynamic-instantiation-from-string-name-of-a-class-in-dynamically-imported

It will also allow us to automatically support new NearNeighbor algorithms ,etc.

2. Consider having more guidance to presets. e.g. instead of just a billion presets, to do more like what the ElementProperty presets and do and just give a few "vetted" suggestions.

issue_title: Add function to verify what Pulp3 plugin is installed.
issue_body:
For Pulp2 before tests related to a certain plugin run, it is verified if the plugin is installed.

See: [get_unit_type_id](https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/blob/00ad3dd55d87f74f8c68e703181686d5dddf9c5d/pulp_smash/utils.py#L452)

In negative cases the test will be skipped. [skip](https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/blob/00ad3dd55d87f74f8c68e703181686d5dddf9c5d/pulp_smash/utils.py#L433)

Pulp3 does not provide information about what plugin is currently installed. However,  such feature will be available in a near future, and the same approach used for Pulp2 plugins should be applied for Pulp3 plugins.

issue_title: Prediction Support
issue_body:
Hello, I just got everything up and running and am currently working on a project with Deep Q learning.  I like how the train_cartpole.py and enjoy_cartpole.py are formatted and it seems easy to tweak parameters and load and save models.

However, what I need to do is train a model and then instead of running it in an interactive game environment, I would like to pass the data for a single observation and get the best prediction of what action I should take.

It doesn't look like the APIs are well setup for this kind of thing, from what I can see.

Do you have any examples of how I would do this ??   Is it possible without hacking into the baselines core code ??

Thanks.

issue_title: Bridge Django field choices and graphene's enum type
issue_body:
It would be awesome to see something like this:

``` py
class Foo(models.Model):
    TWITTER = 'twitter'
    WEBSITE = 'website'
    FULL_NAME = 'full_name'
    IDENTITY_CHOICES = (
        (TWITTER, 'Twitter'),
        (WEBSITE, 'Website'),
        (FULL_NAME, 'Full Name'),
    )

    main_identity = models.CharField(max_length=10, choices=IDENTITY_CHOICES, default=FULL_NAME)
```

turned into something like this:

``` py
class Identity(graphene.Enum):
    TWITTER = Foo.TWITTER
    WEBSITE = Foo.WEBSITE
    FULL_NAME = Foo.FULL_NAME


class FooType(DjangoObjectType):
    main_identity = graphene.NonNull(Identity)

    class Meta:
        model = Foo
```

automatically. I will work on this if I have time to implement it.

issue_body: Since we keep people logged in for 8 hours now, there are times when we need to logout and login again with a different net_id in that timeframe.

issue_title: Make it clearer when no runs are selected
issue_body: When there are many runs, TensorBoard automatically disables all of them. This can make for a confusing experience when you open a category and find nothing present, because all of the runs were disabled. It would be nice if we provided some explicit feedback like "hey, every run is disabled right now, maybe turn some on."

issue_body:

> 
> ![tensorboard dropdown issue](https://github.com/markostam/sandbox/blob/master/photos/Screenshot%202017-05-05%2011.39.26.png?raw=true)
> 
> ### Source code / logs
> Sample of ```metadata.tsv``` file:
> ```
> Name	Genre
> (Sandy) Alex G	Alternative/Indie Rock	
> *NSYNC	Pop/Rock	
> Acollective	Pop/Rock	
> Ahmet Özhan	International	
> Ahu	Club/Dance	
> Alex Ferreira	Pop/Rock	
> Alex Winston	Pop/Rock	
> Ali Azimi	Pop/Rock

> Yomo Toro	Latin
> Youssou N'Dour	International

> Code to generate embeddings and metadata:
> ```python
> def list_to_tsv(filenames, metadata_dir):
>     with open(os.path.join(metadata_dir,'metadata.tsv'), 'w') as tsvfile:
>         writer = csv.writer(tsvfile, delimiter = "\t")
>         for record in filenames:

issue_title: Crear método de generación de indicadores más sencillo, para un sólo catálogo
issue_body:
**Contexto**

El método `generate_catalogs_indicators()` es de uso muy amplio y orientado a generar indicadores de seguimiento de una red de nodos.

Su output es difícil de usar cuando lo único que interesa es tener indicadores del catálogo que está en el objeto DataJson().

Se desea tener un método que pueda ser eventualmente usado para implementar un `/indicators.json` en Portal Andino, que permita descargarse indicadores numéricos del portal.

**Propuesta**

Implementar wrapper `generate_indicators()` que usa `generate_catalogs_indicators()` para devolver sólo un `dict` con los indicadores del catálogo individual del objeto `DataJson()`.

A su vez, implementar los wrappers de indicadores numéricos y no numéricos:

Implementar wrappers que usen estos flags:

+ `generate_numeric_indicators()`
+ `generate_non_numeric_indicators()`

issue_title: Suggested reorganizing of examples filenames
issue_body:
The current sequential numbering of examples makes it a bit of a nuisance to add to in PRs because of example filename conflicts; e.g. multiple PRs adding a new feature each add a new `example20.{cpp,ref,py}`, but that means they can't be merged as is--instead one has to be merged, the other has to be updated to rename the examples, then it can be merged.

(This bit me, twice, in the implicit-cpp-convert PR, which started with example18, but needed to be renamed to example19, then example20 (and might need more renames if some other example-adding PR is accepted in the meantime).  Squashing and rebasing the commits on current master was an even more painful process because every squashed commit to the eventually-renamed example18 resulted in a rebase failure that had to be manually resolved.)

I'd like to propose restructuring the examples with names of what they do, following a `example-some-purpose.{cpp,py,ref}` naming scheme.  I'm happy to do the work, if you are okay with this, just looking for the go-ahead here.  In addition to making PR submissions less conflict-prone, this also has two other advantages that I can see:

- Example filenames are self-documenting
- Test failure out is similarly self-documenting as to which feature is failing

It has the disadvantage of reordering the tests; though if some tests should definitely run first, I suppose they could be named something like "example-000-some-test".

I suggest, as a tentative first step, the following renaming:

example1 -> example-methods-and-attributes
example2 -> example-python-types
example3 -> example-operator-overloading
example4 -> example-constants-and-functions
example5 -> (`*` see below)
example6 -> example-sequence-and-iterators
example7 -> example-buffers
example8 -> example-custom-ref-counting
example9 -> example-modules
example10 -> example-numpy-vectorize
example11 -> example-arg-keywords-and-defaults
example12 -> example-virtual-functions
example13 -> example-keep-alive
example14 -> example-opaque-types
example15 -> example-pickling
example16 -> example-upcasting (`*` or example-inheritance, see below)
example17 -> example-stl-binders
example18 -> example-eval
example19 -> example-custom-exceptions

`*` example5 contains three basically unrelated concepts.  The inheritance parts could be moved into example16 (calling it example-inheritance instead of example-upcasting), the callbacks could go into a new example-callbacks, and the gil into example-gil.

issue_title: fast_cut在句子过长时遇到异常
issue_body:
在Python 3.6下，`fast_cut`在句子过长时会遇到以下异常：
```python
UnicodeDecodeError                        Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-74-be04fb1fbb59> in <module>()
----> 1 len(t.fast_cut(u"...一段很长的话..."))

~/.pyenv/versions/3.6.3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/thulac/__init__.py in fast_cut(self, oiraw, text)
    108
    109     def fast_cut(self, oiraw, text = False):
--> 110         return self.__cutWithOutMethod(oiraw, self.__fast_cutline, text = text)
    111
    112     def __cutline(self, oiraw):

~/.pyenv/versions/3.6.3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/thulac/__init__.py in __cutWithOutMethod(self, oiraw, cut_method, text)
     98                         array += (reduce(lambda x, y: x + [[y, '']], cut_method(line), []))
     99                     else:
--> 100                         array += (reduce(lambda x, y: x + [[y[0], y[2]]], cut_method(line), []))
    101                 array += [['\n', '']]
    102             return array[:-1]

~/.pyenv/versions/3.6.3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/thulac/__init__.py in __fast_cutline(self, oiraw)
    169         if(not self.__so):
    170             self.__so = self.__SoInit()
--> 171         result = self.__so.seg(oiraw)
    172         return result.split()
    173

~/.pyenv/versions/3.6.3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/thulac/manage/SoExtention.py in seg(self, data)
     27         if(isPython2):
     28             return d
---> 29         return s.value.decode('utf-8')

UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0x90 in position 8: invalid start byte
```

初步判断问题在于`SoExtension.py`中：
```python
    def seg(self, data):
        r = self._lib.seg(c_char_p(fixCCP(data)))
        assert r > 0
        self._lib.getResult.restype = POINTER(c_char)
        p = self._lib.getResult()
        s = cast(p,c_char_p)
        d = '%s'%s.value
        self._lib.freeResult();
        if(isPython2):
            return d
        return s.value.decode('utf-8')
```
在`isPython2`为`False`的情况下，会先释放内存再访问`s.value`。可以将最后五行改为：
```python
        if isPython2:
            d = str(s.value)
        else:
            d = s.value.decode('utf-8')
        self._lib.freeResult();
        return d
```
已验证可以解决问题。

issue_title: Model.writeProblem() causes TypeError (version 1.4.3 with Python 3)
issue_body:
# What is problem?

From the version 1.4.3, `Model.writeProblem()` (in `scip.pyx`) causes `TypeError` with Python 3.

The reason is as follows:

1. For Python 3, `str_conversion` is defined as (line 31)
>    str_conversion = lambda x:bytes(x,'utf-8')

2. In the definition of `writeProblem()` the first three lines are (line 741-743)
>    fn = str_conversion(filename)
>    fname, fext = splitext(fn)
>    ext = str_conversion(fext)

3. The default value of `filename='origprob.cip'`. After running the first and the second line we may have `fname=b'origprob'` and `fext=b'.cip'`.

4. How about the third line? It is `str_conversion(fext)`, that is, `bytes(b'.cip','utf-8')` which generates the following error, because an extra encoding `utf-8` was given for the binary array.
> TypeError: encoding without a string argument

# How to solve?

It's simple. Just replace `ext = str_conversion(fext)` (line 743) with `ext = fext`.

issue_title: [proposal] Move dataset download / integrity functions to utils.py

Datasets such as CIFAR / MNIST / etc have download / integrity logic that is useful for reimplementing custom user datasets (such as https://github.com/vadimkantorov/metriclearningbench/blob/master/cars196.py, https://github.com/vadimkantorov/metriclearningbench/blob/master/cub2011.py, https://github.com/vadimkantorov/metriclearningbench/blob/master/stanford_online_products.py)

I propose moving it to `torchvision/datasets/utils.py` functions like downloading and extracting tarballs / zipfiles / plain files; checking integrity by md5 of a file list if it is provided.

issue_title: Label width style attribute
issue_body:
One of the main reasons we need a Label widget is to set the width of the label of a control. Instead, we should just make the label width a style attribute of all labeled widgets (and control the indent property of the checkbox).

- [ ] Move LabeledDOMWidget to a new file
- [ ] Make a new LabeledDOMStyle class that has an attribute for the label width
  - [ ] Should we make minwidth/maxwidth/width properties?
  - [ ] Should we just make a width property, and change the current labels to just have a set width, rather than a min/max width?
- [ ] Make labeled dom widget subclasses styles inherit from the labeleddomstyle

I think we may be able to deprecate the label widget then? Most other uses for the label widget should be shifted to the HTML or HTMLMath widget.

We might also address the discrepancy between "description" and "label". In the code we call it label (that's the html element), but the widget attribute is called description. Does anyone have a preference for one or the other? Label is much shorter, but I'm happy to call it description for backwards compatibility. For example, should we call the style property `description_width` or `label_width`?

CC @SylvainCorlay, @maartenbreddels.

issue_title: Updating dropdown.options does not update value or label
issue_body:

This updates the dropdown *visually* for the user, but apparently not programmatically.  If the user does not make a choice, the `.value` is still the stub value we shoved in by default (`"  -"`). issue_title: Wrap a function to return `Outcome`
issue_body:
The current `capture` API helps a caller of a function turn it from return value/exception to Outcome.

Sometimes a function itself wants to return `Outcome`. One way to do it is to return `Value`, `Error` directly. Another way, safer and more convenient, is to write the function in the usual style and then wrap it with `Outcome`. A decorator is natural for that.

  File "/home/annawoodard/parsl/parsl/executors/ipp.py", line 4, in <module>

Setuptools is supposed to take care of dependencies for us, but importing parsl in `setup.py` breaks that (because we require the dependencies by importing the parsl version from `version.py` before they can be installed). We should avoid this.

issue_title: Make there be a single `script_dir` definition
issue_body:
Reported by @djf604.

It is confusing that we have `script_dir` defined both in the `Provider` and the `Channel`. They do have two different meanings: one is the place we store the scripts _locally_, the other is _remotely_. I vote we remove the confusion by hardcoding the `Provider` script_dir to be a directory within `Config.run_dir`.

```
import os

from localstack.services import infra
from localstack.utils.aws import aws_stack

class TestPredictionStream(object):

    SNS_ENDPOINT_URL = LOCALSTACK_HOST + ':' + SNS_PORT

    TEST_QUEUE_NAME = 'test_queue'

    @classmethod
    def setup_class(cls):
        services_needed_for_test = ','.join([
            'sqs:' + TestPredictionStream.SQS_PORT,
            'sns:' + TestPredictionStream.SNS_PORT
        ])
        os.environ['SERVICES'] = services_needed_for_test
        infra.start_infra(async=True)

    @classmethod
    def teardown_class(cls):

    def setup(self):
        self.sqs_client = aws_stack.connect_to_service('sqs')
        self.sns_client = aws_stack.connect_to_service('sns')
        self.queue_info = self._setup_queue()
        self.topic_info = self._setup_topic()

    def _setup_queue(self):
        self.sqs_client.create_queue(QueueName=self.TEST_QUEUE_NAME)

    def _setup_topic(self):
        return self.sns_client.create_topic(Name=self.TEST_SNS_TOPIC)

    def teardown(self):
        self._teardown_queue()
        self._teardown_topic()

    def _teardown_queue(self):
        self.sqs_client.delete_queue(QueueUrl=self.TEST_QUEUE_NAME)

    def _teardown_topic(self):
        self.sns_client.delete_topic(TopicArn=self.topic_info['TopicArn'])

issue_title: implement .get_metadata for DockerImage
Add N checksum (already existing on C)... useful for partitioned files...
issue_body:
replace checksums on 0, L and R packets with replicable algorithm.

currently when generating checksum fields for items with certain checksum: 0, Link, or Remove, the checksum value is a random integer, to avoid load all going to a single node when there is load splitting (such as post_exchange_split ) It would be better if the load splitting were predictable, as well as distributed, so that files with the same name would go to the same node. First idea: a good way of accomplishing that would be to change the algorithms to use the 'n' algorithm... ie. take an md5 checksum of the filename as the value.

That way the computation is reproducible. for 'R', that should work well. for Link, it would be even better to have the checksum be based on the link content. so that the checksum will change if the link ever does.

Even the 'n' algorithm, as is, has a problem. If a file is partitioned, all of the parts will go to the same node (as they have the same checksum.) It is probably important to include the partition information in the checksum calculation. Doing so would also make it perfect for use by sr_winnow.

So, perhaps ideally, we create an 'N' algorithm, which concatenates name, and the 'parts' header,
and uses the checksum of that string.

Implemented in the C-side. (in 2017/07) never made it to the python side yet... hence the Issue.
